criteria: The agent has read Section 2 completely to understand the crystal representation (A, P, L) matrices and the multi-edge graph construction method for capturing atom interactions across cell boundaries.
The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand the formal definitions of Unit Cell E(3) Invariance (Definition 1) and Periodic Invariance (Definition 2).
The agent has read Section 3.2 completely to understand why the lattice matrix L encoding periodic patterns must be explicitly encoded in crystal representations.
The agent has read Section 4.1 completely to understand the radius-based multi-edge and fully connected graph construction methods and the periodic pattern encoding with six self-connecting edges.

The agent has read Section 4.2 completely to understand the three-step message passing scheme: edge-wise attention coefficient computing (Equation 2), edge-wise value message computing (Equation 3), and node updating (Equation 4).
The agent has read Table 1 to check the expected test MAE results of Matformer and all baselines on The Materials Project dataset.
The agent has read Table 2 to check the expected test MAE results of Matformer and all baselines on the JARVIS dataset.
The agent has read Table 3 to check the efficiency comparison (training time per epoch, total training time, inference time, model parameters) between Matformer and ALIGNN on JARVIS formation energy.
The agent has read Table 4 to check the EwT (Energy within Threshold) results comparing Matformer and ALIGNN on JARVIS formation energy, JARVIS total energy, and Materials Project formation energy.

The agent has read Table 5 to check the ablation study results on periodic invariance and periodic pattern encoding, including configurations for OCgraph, Radius w/o PE, T-fully w PE, and Radius w PE.
The agent has read Table 6 to check the ablation results on adding angular information (Matformer + Angle SBF, Matformer + Angle RBF) in terms of MAE and training time.
The agent has read Appendix A.3 to check the detailed dataset descriptions and training/validation/test split sizes for The Materials Project and JARVIS.

The agent has read Appendix A.4 to check the detailed Matformer configurations including node embedding, edge embedding, layer settings, attention heads, and task-specific training hyperparameters (Tables 7 and 8).
The agent has read Appendix A.6 to check the ablation study comparing sigmoid+layernorm versus softmax variants in the message passing layer (Table 10).
The plan includes the representation of each crystal as (A, P, L) where A is the atom feature matrix, P is the position matrix, and L is the 3x3 lattice matrix describing repeating directions.
The plan includes the radius-based multi-edge graph construction where the radius is set to the 12th smallest pairwise distance for each atom, forming all edges within that radius.
The plan includes the periodic pattern encoding by adding six self-connecting edges per node with distances ||ℓ₁||₂, ||ℓ₂||₂, ||ℓ₃||₂, ||ℓ₁+ℓ₂||₂, ||ℓ₂+ℓ₃||₂, and ||ℓ₁+ℓ₃||₂.

The plan includes the three-step Matformer message passing: edge-wise attention coefficients without softmax, sigmoid-gated value messages with layer normalization, and batch-normalized node feature updates.
The plan includes node embedding using 92-dimensional CGCNN atomic embedding mapped to 128-dimensional input feature vectors via a linear transformation.
The plan includes edge embedding using 128 RBF kernels with centers from 0.0 to 8.0 and a nonlinear layer followed by a linear layer to produce 128-dimensional edge features.
The plan includes five Matformer message passing layers with four attention heads and hidden embedding dimension of 128.
The plan includes the readout layer using mean pooling over node features followed by a nonlinear layer with hidden dimension 128 and a final linear layer for scalar output.
The plan includes experiments on The Materials Project dataset with four tasks: formation energy (eV/atom), band gap (eV), bulk moduli (log GPa), and shear moduli (log GPa).
The plan includes experiments on the JARVIS dataset with five tasks: formation energy, bandgap(OPT), total energy, Ehull, and bandgap(MBJ).
The plan includes the implementation of all baseline methods (CGCNN, SchNet, MEGNET, GATGNN, ALIGNN) for fair comparison.
The plan includes the ablation studies comparing OCgraph vs Radius construction and with/without periodic pattern encoding on JARVIS formation energy.
The plan includes training with Adam optimizer, weight decay of 1e-5, one cycle learning rate scheduler, and batch size of 64, with MSE loss and MAE evaluation metric.
The plan includes the EwT (Energy within Threshold) evaluation metric with thresholds of 0.01 and 0.02 for comparison with ALIGNN.
The code for representing the infinite crystal structure using Equation 1 (all atom positions as p_i + k1*ℓ1 + k2*ℓ2 + k3*ℓ3 for k1, k2, k3 ∈ Z) has been implemented.

The code for the radius-based multi-edge graph construction has been implemented, where the radius r for each atom is set to the 12th smallest pairwise distance between that atom and all nearby atoms.

The code correctly builds multiple edges from node j to node i when multiple positions of atom j fall within the radius of atom i, where each edge has the corresponding Euclidean distance d'_ji as its initial feature.

The code for the fully connected graph construction (T-fully) with t=3 smallest pairwise distances between every pair of nodes i and j in {d_ij | d_ij = ||p_i - p_j + k1'ℓ1 + k2'ℓ2 + k3'ℓ3||₂} has been implemented.
The code for periodic pattern encoding has been implemented by adding six self-connecting edges per node with distances ||ℓ₁||₂, ||ℓ₂||₂, ||ℓ₃||₂, ||ℓ₁+ℓ₂||₂, ||ℓ₂+ℓ₃||₂, and ||ℓ₁+ℓ₃||₂.
The code correctly omits adding a self-connecting edge if its corresponding distance is already within the computed radius of node i from the radius-based graph construction step.

The code for node embedding has been implemented by mapping atomic number to a 92-dimensional CGCNN atomic embedding and then applying a linear transformation to produce a 128-dimensional input feature vector f_i*.

The code for edge embedding has been implemented by mapping Euclidean distance to a 128-dimensional vector using 128 RBF kernels with centers from 0.0 to 8.0, followed by a nonlinear (Softplus) layer and a linear layer to produce 128-dimensional edge features e_ij^h.

The code for Step 1 of Matformer message passing (Equation 2) has been implemented: computing q_i, k_i, k_j using separate linear transformations LN_Q and LN_K, computing e_ij^{h'} via LN_E, forming q_ij^h by concatenating three copies of q_i, forming k_ij^h by concatenating k_i, k_j, and e_ij^{h'}, and computing α_ij^h via Hadamard product divided by sqrt(d_k).
The code correctly omits softmax in the attention coefficient computation to preserve the ability to distinguish nodes with different degrees in multi-edge crystal graphs.

The code for Step 2 of Matformer message passing (Equation 3) has been implemented: computing v_i and v_j via LN_V, computing m_ij^h as the Hadamard product of sigmoid(LNorm(α_ij^h)) and LN_update(v_i | v_j | e_ij^{h'}).

The code for Step 3 of Matformer message passing (Equation 4) has been implemented: computing the aggregate message m_i by summing LNorm(LN_msg(m_ij^h)) over all neighbors j in N_i and all edges h, and updating node feature f_i^ℓ as LN_fea(f_i^{*ℓ}) + σ(BN(m_i)).

The code for the full Matformer architecture with five message passing layers and four attention heads has been implemented, with each head using separate LN_Q, LN_K, LN_V, and LN_E transformations but sharing other operations.

The code for concatenating features from four attention heads and projecting the concatenated output to a 128-dimensional vector via a linear transformation to obtain the final message m_i has been implemented.

The code for the readout layer has been implemented: mean pooling over all node feature vectors in the crystal graph, followed by a nonlinear layer with hidden dimension 128 and a linear layer to produce the scalar output.

The code for loading The Materials Project dataset with the correct training/validation/test splits has been implemented: 60000/5000/4239 for formation energy and band gap; 4664/393/393 for bulk moduli and shear moduli.

The code for loading the JARVIS dataset with correct training/validation/test splits has been implemented: 44578/5572/5572 for formation energy, total energy, and bandgap(OPT); 44296/5537/5537 for Ehull; 14537/1817/1817 for bandgap(MBJ).

The code for training with Adam optimizer with weight decay of 1e-5 and one cycle learning rate scheduler using task-specific learning rates (Table 7 and Table 8) and batch size of 64 has been implemented.
The code for training with MSE (mean square error) as the objective loss function and evaluating with MAE (mean absolute error) as the metric has been implemented.
The code for the EwT (Energy within Threshold) metric has been implemented, measuring the percentage of predicted energies with absolute error within thresholds of 0.01 and 0.02.
The code for all baseline methods (CGCNN, SchNet, MEGNET, GATGNN, ALIGNN) has been implemented or configured using official code bases with the same dataset splits.
The code for the ablation study with Graphormer-style OCgraph construction (treating each atom as a single node in a fully connected graph) has been implemented for comparison.
The code for the ablation study comparing Matformer variants (Softmax-scalar, Softmax-vector, Sigmoid+Norm) in the message passing layer has been implemented.

The code for the angular information ablation (Matformer + Angle RBF using Radial Basis Function kernels and Matformer + Angle SBF using Spherical Bessel Functions with Spherical Harmonics) has been implemented with two extra Matformer layers for angular processing.
The code for Matformer on all four tasks of The Materials Project has been executed successfully without errors.
The code for Matformer on all five tasks of the JARVIS dataset has been executed successfully without errors.
The code for all baseline methods (CGCNN, SchNet, MEGNET, GATGNN, ALIGNN) on The Materials Project and JARVIS datasets has been executed successfully without errors.
The ablation experiments comparing OCgraph, Radius w/o PE (3 layers / 5 layers), T-fully w PE, and Radius w PE on JARVIS formation energy have been executed successfully.
The EwT metric evaluation comparing Matformer and ALIGNN on JARVIS formation energy, JARVIS total energy, and Materials Project formation energy has been executed successfully.

The efficiency comparison experiment measuring training time per epoch, total training time, inference time, and model parameters for Matformer and ALIGNN on JARVIS formation energy has been executed successfully.
The building block ablation experiments for Softmax-scalar, Softmax-vector, and Sigmoid+Norm on JARVIS formation energy have been executed successfully.
The angular information ablation experiments (Matformer, Matformer + Angle SBF, Matformer + Angle RBF) have been executed successfully with timing recorded.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 1: Matformer achieves the best performance on all four Materials Project tasks (formation energy: 0.021 eV/atom, band gap: 0.211 eV, bulk moduli: 0.043 log(GPa), shear moduli: 0.073 log(GPa)).

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 2: Matformer achieves the best performance on all five JARVIS tasks (formation energy: 0.0325, bandgap(OPT): 0.137, total energy: 0.035, Ehull: 0.064, bandgap(MBJ): 0.30 eV).

The execution outcomes match Table 3: Matformer is approximately 3x faster than ALIGNN in training time (64 s/epoch vs 327 s/epoch), total training time (8.9 h vs 27.3 h), and inference time (59 s vs 156 s), with smaller model size (11.0 MB vs 15.4 MB).

The execution outcomes match Table 4: Matformer outperforms ALIGNN on EwT metric for all three energy tasks, with Formation MP EwT(0.01)=55.86% vs 49.94%, Formation JARVIS EwT(0.01)=41.17% vs 39.59%, and Total JARVIS EwT(0.01)=36.84% vs 35.09%.

The execution outcomes match Table 5 ablation results: OCgraph gives test MAE 0.0530; Radius w/o PE (3 layers, 1 head) gives 0.0348; Radius w/o PE (5 layers, 4 heads) gives 0.0337; T-fully w PE gives 0.0402; Radius w PE gives 0.0325.
The execution outcomes confirm that using OCgraph increases test MAE from 0.0325 to 0.0530 (~53% increase), demonstrating the critical importance of periodic invariant graph construction.

The execution outcomes confirm that omitting periodic pattern encoding (PE) increases test MAE from 0.0325 to 0.0337 on JARVIS formation energy, demonstrating the importance of the self-connecting edge encoding.

The execution outcomes match Table 6: adding angular information does not significantly improve MAE (Angle RBF: 0.0325, Angle SBF: 0.0332 vs Matformer: 0.0325) while increasing training time ~3x (165 s and 173 s per epoch vs 64 s).

The execution outcomes match Table 10: Sigmoid+Norm achieves test MAE of 0.0325, outperforming Softmax-vector (0.0347) and Softmax-scalar (0.0376), confirming the effectiveness of sigmoid+layernorm over softmax in Matformer message passing.

The execution outcomes confirm that Matformer outperforms ALIGNN on JARVIS Ehull by 0.012 (0.064 vs 0.076), which is a 15.8% reduction, consistent with the paper's claims about periodic pattern encoding advantage.
The execution outcomes confirm that Matformer achieves the best performance on Materials Project formation energy with a 4.5% reduction (0.021 vs 0.022) compared to the second best model ALIGNN.
